rulings.Case Title: DONALD NORMAN VS. PATRICK STRATEMAN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff'S Motion For Leave To Amend Per Order Filed 10/9/19)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, October 18, 2019, Line 12, Plaintiff'S Motion For Leave To Amend Per Order Filed 10/9/19). Plaintiff Donald Norman's "application for an order allowing plaintiff's experts access to discovery" is denied. Plaintiff elected to sue in San Francisco and suggests no reason why his disclosed damages expert, Carl Saba, and Saba's team cannot conduct, in San Francisco, any further review of the subject database that is needed. Moreover, plaintiff's description of what five people in Croatia and Serbia would do with the database is sparse at best. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: JOSEY RUSSELL ET AL VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT , Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, October 18, 2019, Line 3, DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT , Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication. Defendant City and County of San Francisco's motion for summary judgment is granted. The causes of action for gender/sexual orientation discrimination, retaliation, failure to maintain environment free of discrimination/harassment, and race discrimination all fail because plaintiffs cannot demonstrate an adverse employment action. Plaintiffs also fail to demonstrate harassment as they cite isolated and sporadic remarks. To establish a discrimination or retaliation claim, "[t]he employment action must be both detrimental and substantial." (Thomas v. Department of Corrections (2000) 77 Cal.App.4th 507, 511; see also McRae v. Dep't of Corr. & Rehab. (2005) 142 Cal.App.4th 377, 386-392 [memoranda in file and transfer not adverse employment action]; Akers v. County of San Diego (2002) 95 Cal.App.4th 1441, 1457 ["We agree that a mere oral or written criticism of an employee or a transfer into a comparable position does not meet the definition of an adverse employment action under FEHA."].) In this case, there is no evidence that the internal affairs investigations and/or discipline made plaintiffs unpromotable (like in Akers). (Houston Depo., 27:14-28:2.) Plaintiff Mohamed maintained her same rank, pay and duties when she was transferred. (Mohamed Depo., 190:23-192:5.) Plaintiff Russell similarly was not detrimentally harmed by the discipline. (UMF 59.) There is no evidence that Chief Scott harbored any animus towards plaintiffs and plaintiffs do not establish a "cat's paw" theory of liability as numerous persons were involved with plaintiffs' discipline. (UMF 41, 46, 51, 56, 61; Mohamed Depo. 192:14-17.) The court further concludes that plaintiff Mohamed failed to exhaust the race discrimination cause of action. While the gender discrimination, sexual orientation discrimination, and failure to prevent claims would likely be unearthed during the course of the DFEH investigation, the race discrimination claim is distinct. (See Okoli v. Lockheed Technical Operations Co. (1995) 36 Cal.App.4th 1607.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for CCSF is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: BANK OF AMERICA, N.A. VS. MICHAEL LIPSON ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, October 18, 2019, Line 5, DEFENDANT MICHAEL LIPSON's DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. Despite this court's prior order, counsel apparently have still failed to comply with CCP §430.41. That statute requires a meet and confer "in person or by telephone" to determine "whether an agreement can be reached that would resolve the objections to be raised in the demurrer." Plaintiff's counsel declares that a phone call occurred, but defendant's counsel "did not specify what causes of action to which he intended to demur, nor did he provide the basis on which he intended to demur." Defendant's counsel's declaration is not to the contrary. Counsel are once again admonished to obey CCP §430.41 and the demurrer is ordered off calendar. The parties are ordered to meet and confer immediately. Defendant has until November 18, 2019 to respond to the first amended complaint. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: BAY AREA DIAMOND WHOLESALERS, INC. VS. C.H.A CORP ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, October 18, 2019, Line 11, DEFENDANT C.H.A CORP, GIDEON CHAMDI's DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT. Defendants' demurrers to the second amended complaint are overruled in their entirety. Causes of action 1 (breach of written contract), 3 (specific performance), 7 (Bus. & Prof. Code §17200) and 8 (fraud) do not differ between the first and second amended complaints and demurrers to them were already overruled on September 26, 2018. No reconsideration motion was filed. (Benefit v. Suncloud 1997) 56 Cal.App.4th 91, 96-97.) As to other causes of action: 2 (breach of oral agreement), 4 (account stated) and 5 (open book) are all appropriately pled in the alternative. 6 (voidable transaction): adequately pled. 10 (breach of Corporations Code §§1903, 2004): private rights of action exist, and breach is adequately pled. 11 (violation of Civil Code §3439.04): alter ego adequately pled. 12: (Bus. & Prof. Code §17200): adequately pled. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the Plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: REGIONAL PROTECTION AGENCY, INC. VS. UBER TECHNOLOGIES, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Strike Portions Of Plaintiffs Verified Second Amended Complaint For Damagesverified Complaint For Damages
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, October 18, 2019, Line 13, DEFENDANT UBER TECHNOLOGIES, INC., REYNALDO PEREZ, NAVARR HANNON's Motion To Strike Portions Of Plaintiffs Verified Second Amended Complaint For Damagesverified Complaint For Damages. Defendants' motion to strike portions of the second amended complaint (SAC) is denied. The SAC sufficiently pleads facts supporting punitive damages prayers. (See, e.g., SAC 3:18-21, 4:8-5:2, 5:7-6:20; Civ. Code §3294; Perkins v. Sup. Ct. (1981) 117 Cal.App.3d 1, 6.) rulings.Case Title: PRAYOOT JANTARADEJDUANGDEE VS. BILLION MICRO, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Prayoot Jantaradejduangdee'S Motion For Terminating Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: October 18, 2019 Line 1, Plaintiff Prayoot Jantaradejduangdee'S Motion For Terminating Sanctions Pro Tem Judge Katherine Gallo, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion for terminating sanctions against Mongkol Jaemjaeng is granted. Mongkol Jaemjaeng's answer is stricken and the court enters a default. The motion for terminating sanctions is denied as to Chonthida S. Jaemjaeng and Billion Micro, Inc. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to klgallo@discoveryreferee.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. (jpt/302)

rulings.Case Title: ARMANDO REYES VS. STARBUCKS CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion, Motion For Order Compelling Defendant Starbucks Corporation And Jen Lu To (1) Further Respond To Plaintiff'S Request For Admissions And For (2) An Award Of Serious Monetary Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: October 18, 2019 Line 3, PLAINTIFF ARMANDO REYES' Motion For Order Compelling Defendant Starbucks Corporation And Jen Lu To (1) Further Respond To Plaintiff'S Request For Admissions And For (2) An Award Of Serious Monetary Sanctions The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion is denied. Plaintiff's memorandum of points and authorities exceeds fifteen pages and is in violation of California Rules of Court Rule 3.1113(e) and is being treated as late papers pursuant to California Rules of Court Rule 3.1113(g). Plaintiff also did not provide table of contents or table of authorities as is required by California Rules of Court Rule 3.1113(f) Plaintiff failed to provide a separate statement of items in dispute as required by California Rules of Court Rule 3.1345(a) and (c). Plaintiff's motion is also untimely as it was brought more than 45 days of service of verified responses. Defendant's request for sanctions is denied as the Declaration of Emily G. Genge fails to describe in detail what work she performed, the number of hours each task took or will take (i.e., preparing motion, travel to court etc.), her hourly rate and the costs incurred. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to klgallo@discoveryreferee.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. (jpt/302).
